justification: All 6 required rules identified (plus a 7th reinforcing license verification at the physician lifecycle level), all with correct invariant classification. Cancellation rules correctly split into BR-3 (patient, invariant: false for the time threshold) and BR-4 (admin override, invariant: false), capturing the role boundary. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum), covering all required scenarios: licensed physician booking (AC-1, AC-12), double-booking attempt (AC-2, AC-3), late cancellation attempt (AC-4, AC-5, AC-11), admin cancellation (AC-6), and unauthorized record access (AC-7, AC-8). Confidence level of 82 falls within the 65-90 range, with clearly stated assumptions about license validation semantics, interval handling, and boundary conditions.

However, BR-6 (consultation notes 48h lock) is classified as invariant: false, while the rubric requires it as invariant: true (clinical record integrity / regulatory). This is 1 invariant misclassification. License verification (BR-1) and record visibility (BR-5) correctly marked invariant: true. 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the 4 minimum. Role-based cancellation correctly captured. Confidence at 72 with well-stated assumptions about regulatory framework, treating physician scope, and boundary conditions. The cancellation role boundary (BR-3/BR-4) being marked invariant: false is debatable — the rubric considers the role boundary itself as invariant: true, which is a second arguable misclassification.

observations.text: All 6 expected business rules identified and clearly articulated with testable assertions
10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria — far exceeds the 4 minimum, covering booking, double-booking, both cancellation roles, record access (authorized and unauthorized), and notes locking

Role-based cancellation distinction between patient and admin correctly captured as separate rules (BR-3, BR-4)
Confidence level 72 is within the 65-90 range with well-reasoned assumptions about regulatory framework ambiguity and treating physician scope
